course_description:
This course will cover a selection of topics from the areas of combinatorial geometry, graph theory, design theory, and probabilistic combinatorics.

This course provides students with an introduction to various areas of combinatorics including combinatorial geometry, design theory and graph theory. We will encounter Pick's Theorem, Sperner's Lemma, incidence structures, projective and affine geometries, Steiner systems, permutation groups, graph symmetries, the Erdos-Ko-Rado Theorem, and the Hoffman-Singleton Theorem.

It should enable students to read more widely in these areas, and apply their knowledge to other appropriate fields.

In the School of Mathematics and Physics we are committed to creating an inclusive and empowering learning environment for all students. We value and respect the diverse range of experiences our students bring to their education, and we believe that this diversity is crucial for fostering a rich culture of knowledge sharing and meaningful exploration. We hold both students and staff accountable for actively contributing to the establishment of a respectful and supportive learning environment.

Bullying, harassment, and discrimination in any form are strictly against our principles and against UQ Policy, and will not be tolerated. We have developed a suite of resources to assist you in recognising, reporting, and addressing such behaviour. If you have any concerns about your experience in this course, we encourage you to tell a member of the course teaching team, or alternatively contact an SMP Classroom Inclusivity Champion (see Blackboard for contact details). Our Inclusivity Champions are here to listen, to understand your concerns, and to explore potential actions that can be taken to resolve them. Your well-being and a positive learning atmosphere are of utmost importance to us.

Additional timetable information
All classes will be conducted on campus. Consult your personal timetable for times and locations. Students are expected to attend these sessions unless they have a valid reason for being unable to attend (such as illness).
course_aims: This course will cover a selection of combinatorial mathematics topics from areas such as combinatorial geometry, graph theory, design theory, and probabilistic combinatorics. Building on students’ previous studies in combinatorics, by the end of this course students will have a sufficient understanding of important topics in combinatorics to be able to undertake further reading on these topics through graduate texts and research papers, as necessary for conducting research in these or related areas.

learning_outcomes.description: explain a selection of combinatorial topics and their importance;
construct logical, clearly presented and justified solutions to combinatorial problems;
summarise and interpret existing research on related topics and effectively communicate this information in oral and written format.

assessment_details.title: In-class seminar-style presentation
assessment_details.task_description: A 10-20-minute seminar-style presentation is to be given in the usual lecture or applied class timeslots during week 12 or week 13. The presentation will be followed by questions from the audience. Options for seminar content and topics will be given out and discussed in class. A timetable informing students of the date and time for their talk will be given out in class. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
If unavoidable circumstances prevent you from giving your presentation at all of the available times in weeks 12 and 13, then you'll need to demonstrate through supporting documentation how unavoidable circumstances prevented you from giving your presentation.
Requests to defer your presentation are submitted online via mySi-net. Requests received in any other way will not be approved.
Please note:
Applications can be submitted no later than 5 calendar days after the scheduled date of your presentation. Your request must have a status of "submitted" in mySI-net to be assessed. All applications for deferred presentations are assessed by the relevant school. You’ll receive an email to your student email account when the status of your application is updated.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Classes This course has two 2-hour classes per week. Each 2-hour class will be a combination of lecture and students working on problems with help from the lecturer. Classes will be used to define the course material, set out the basic theory, demonstrate techniques for problem solving, and to provide administrative information for the course. Students are encouraged to answer or ask questions when opportunities arise. There will be opportunity for individual assistance. Students will be given problems to complete during class, giving them the opportunity to work with assistance available from the lecturer, and/or from their peers. The classes also provide an opportunity to ask questions about other aspects of the course.
